region_of_origin: South India (Tamil Nadu / Chennai), adapted in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific Mutton Madras entry; closest match is lamb curry (FOODON_03315671). Mutton Madras is a South Indian-style spiced lamb/mutton curry.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated Mutton Madras code; A0FJP (meat-based dish, curry) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for Mutton Madras curry dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Mutton Madras on a hot tawa plate is approximately 250 g, comprising braised mutton pieces in spiced curry sauce with onions and tomatoes.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Madras — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mutton Madras (cr_id G00010923). All core macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 5.2 g/100 g based on typical mutton + coconut milk composition consistent with HPB data; monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, and trans fat not reported in HPB source and set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Meat Curry Dishes — analogous estimate based on lamb/mutton curry with vegetables
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Mutton Madras in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous meat-based curry dishes with moderate carbohydrate content (7.44 g/100 g), high protein (14.05 g/100 g), and significant fat (11.58 g/100 g), all of which attenuate postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (35 × 7.44 × 2.5) / 100 = 6.5 g. This is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database data for comparable South Asian meat curry preparations.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 467 mg/100 g with low dietary fibre (0.3 g/100 g) and elevated saturated fat (~5.2 g/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced-salt preparation, as a 250 g serving delivers approximately 1168 mg sodium (nearly 50% of the 2400 mg daily limit); pairing with high-potassium, high-fibre sides such as lentils or leafy greens is advised to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (7.44 g/100 g) and sugar (4.69 g/100 g) with high protein (14.05 g/100 g) slowing gastric emptying; saturated fat (~5.2 g/100 g) may worsen insulin resistance with regular consumption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mutton Madras is acceptable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in controlled portions (150–200 g) due to its low GI (~35) and high protein content that blunts postprandial glucose rise, but saturated fat from mutton and coconut milk should be monitored to avoid worsening insulin resistance over time; pair with low-GI carbohydrates such as basmati rice rather than white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat ~5.2 g/100 g, cholesterol 85 mg/100 g, and sodium 467 mg/100 g collectively elevate LDL-C and blood pressure risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Mutton Madras sparingly (maximum 100–150 g per occasion) as the combination of saturated fat (~5.2 g/100 g), dietary cholesterol (85 mg/100 g), and high sodium (467 mg/100 g) contributes to LDL elevation and arterial stiffness; requesting reduced coconut milk and leaner mutton cuts, and pairing with fibre-rich sides, can partially mitigate cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (mutton/lamb) is high in arachidonic acid, amplifying TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways; saturated fat ~5.2 g/100 g further promotes systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit Mutton Madras as mutton is a primary pro-inflammatory red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6); substituting with chicken or fish and requesting reduced coconut milk are recommended to lower inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Madras is a South Indian-origin spiced curry dish popular in Singapore's Indian restaurant and hawker culture. It consists of succulent pieces of mutton (lamb) slow-cooked with chopped onions, tomatoes, and a bold Madras-style spice blend that typically includes turmeric, red chili, coriander, cumin, ginger, garlic, and coconut milk, served sizzling on a hot iron tawa plate. The dish has a rich, deeply spiced, moderately hot gravy with a slightly tangy tomato base and a creamy undertone from coconut milk. The texture of the mutton is tender from braising, and the sauce is thick and aromatic. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 192 kcal, 14.05 g protein, 11.58 g fat (of which approximately 5.2 g is saturated), 7.44 g carbohydrate, 4.69 g sugar, 0.3 g dietary fibre, and 467 mg sodium. The high protein content supports satiety and muscle maintenance, while the moderate carbohydrate level and low GI (~35) make it relatively suitable for blood glucose management in controlled portions. However, the elevated sodium and saturated fat content raise concerns for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. Clinically, it is best consumed in portions of 150–200 g paired with low-GI carbohydrates and high-fibre sides. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore establishments and is gluten-free. It is commonly served at lunch and dinner as a main course alongside rice, roti prata, or naan.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Mutton Madras (cr_id G00010923) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated at ~5.2 g/100 g from typical mutton + coconut milk composition consistent with HPB data. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database comparators, reducing overall confidence for GI component to medium, but the overall record confidence remains high due to exact HPB source match for all primary nutrients.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin profile, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for Mutton Madras are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (G00010923) as of 2026-04-16. 2. saturated_fat_g (5.2 g/100 g) is estimated from typical mutton shoulder/leg composition plus coconut milk contribution, consistent with HPB data context; not directly reported in the HPB record. 3. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database comparators for South Asian meat curry dishes with similar macronutrient profiles; no direct GI measurement for Mutton Madras exists in the database. 4. Halal status reflects standard Singapore Indian restaurant preparation with mutton; individual establishments should be verified. 5. The description notes 'lamb' in the HPB source description — in Singapore culinary context, 'mutton' and 'lamb' are used interchangeably for this dish; the protein is from ovine (sheep/goat) meat.
